The various ways of making change for a dollar using pennies, nickels, dimes,
and quarters may be represented by the members of the following set of 4-tuples
of numbers:

$$
D = \{(p,n,d,q): (1\times p) + (5\times n) + (10\times d) + (25\times q) = 100\}
$$

The members of this set can be given the following inductive definition:

1.  $(0,0,0,4)\in D$.

2.  If $(p,n,d,q)\in D$ and $q> 0$, then $(p,n+1,d+2,q-1)\in D$.

3.  If $(p,n,d,q)\in D$ and $q>1$, then $(p, n, d+5, q-2)\in D$.

4.  If $(p,n,d,q)\in D$ and $d> 0$, then $(p,n+2,d-1,q)\in D$.

5.  If $(p,n,d,q)\in D$ and $n> 0$, then $(p+5, n-1, d, q)\in D$.

Using this inductive definition, show that the following two
claims are true:

(a). There is no way to make change for a dollar from exactly five coins.

(b). There is no way to make change for a dollar from an assortment of coins
in which the total number of non-dimes (i.e., pennies, nickels, and quarters) is odd.
